content:
# Genesis Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4v–5r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_007_page_001.jpg, GEN_007_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009

---

**1** And the LORD said to Noah, "Come into the ark, you and all your household, for I have seen that you are righteous before me in this generation.

**2** From every clean animal, take with you seven pairs, a male and his female; and from the animals that are not clean, two, a male and his female;

**3** also from the birds of the heavens, seven pairs, male and female, to keep seed alive on the face of all the earth.

**4** For in seven more days I will send rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights, and every living thing that I have made I will blot out from the face of the ground."

**5** And Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him.

**6** And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters came upon the earth.

**7** And Noah went in, and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood.

**8** From clean animals and from animals that are not clean, and from birds and from everything that creeps on the ground,

**9** two by two they went into the ark to Noah, male and female, as God had commanded Noah.

**10** And after seven days the waters of the flood came upon the earth.

**11** In the six-hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on that very day all the fountains of the great deep burst open, and the windows of the heavens were opened.

**12** And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights.

**13** On that very day Noah entered, and Shem and Ham and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah's wife and the three wives of his sons with them into the ark —

**14** they and every beast according to its kind, and every livestock according to its kind, and every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth according to its kind, and every bird according to its kind, every winged creature of every kind.

**15** And they went into the ark to Noah, two by two, from all flesh in which there was the breath of life.

**16** And those that came, male and female from all flesh, came as God had commanded him. And the LORD shut him in.

**17** And the flood was forty days upon the earth, and the waters increased and bore up the ark, and it was lifted above the earth.

**18** And the waters prevailed and increased greatly upon the earth, and the ark went on the face of the waters.

**19** And the waters prevailed exceedingly greatly upon the earth, and all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered.

**20** Fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed, and the mountains were covered.

**21** And all flesh expired that moves upon the earth — birds, livestock, beasts, every swarming thing that swarms upon the earth, and every human being.

**22** Everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, from all that was on dry land, died.

**23** And every living thing was blotted out that was on the face of the ground — from human being to beast, to creeping thing, to birds of the heavens — they were blotted out from the earth. And only Noah was left, and those that were with him in the ark.

**24** And the waters prevailed upon the earth one hundred and fifty days.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of three manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 7 spans Folios 4v–5r. Folio 4v (page 001 of this chapter) contains the latter sections of Genesis 6 in its opening columns and runs through approximately Genesis 7:9–10 by the foot of the folio. Folio 5r (page 002 of this chapter) continues from approximately verse 10–11 through verse 24 and into the opening of Genesis 8. The three-column manuscript layout is clear on both folios. The Tiberian vocalization — qamets, patah, tsere, hiriq, shureq, and the full system of cantillation marks (te'amim) — is present and legible throughout both folios. The divine name יְהוָה appears in verses 1, 5, and 16 with the Masoretic qere notation. The divine name אֱלֹהִים appears in verses 9 and 16. The chapter contains the rare juxtaposition of both divine names in a single verse (v. 16), which is a primary textual feature of this chapter. The paragraph (parashah) division markers are visible at the major literary seams.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 7 was read directly from the folio (image labeled GEN_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg; catalogue reference Vat.gr.1209/0009). The folio image carries the chapter number annotation "CAP. VII" in the left margin and "CAP. VIII" at the lower right margin, confirming that Genesis 7 and the opening of Genesis 8 appear on this single folio. The Greek text is written in the characteristic three-column uncial hand of the Vaticanus. The LXX Genesis 7 presents several significant textual expansions and divergences from the MT that are documented in full below, most notably in the clean/unclean animal distinction (v. 2), the flood chronology, and the cosmological language of verse 11.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. יְהוָה / אֱלֹהִים (YHWH / Elohim) — The Divine Name Alternation in Chapter 7**

Genesis 7 is one of the most studied chapters in the Pentateuch for the question of divine-name distribution. The chapter uses both יְהוָה ("the LORD," the Tetragrammaton) and אֱלֹהִים ("God," the generic divine title) — not as random variation but in a discernible pattern:

- יְהוָה (the LORD): verses 1, 5, 16 (first occurrence)
- אֱלֹהִים (God): verses 9, 16 (second occurrence)

The Documentary Hypothesis classically assigns the יְהוָה-sections to the Yahwist (J) source and the אֱלֹהִים-sections to the Priestly (P) source, treating Genesis 7 as one of the clearest examples of source interweaving. On this analysis:

- The J narrative (vv. 1–5, 7–10, 16b, 17, 22–23) features: the LORD's personal command to Noah, the clean/unclean animal distinction, seven pairs of clean animals, a forty-day rain-flood, and the LORD personally shutting Noah in.
- The P narrative (vv. 6, 8–9, 11, 13–16a, 18–21, 24) features: the precise date notation, two-by-two animals without the clean/unclean distinction, the cosmological language of the fountains of the deep and windows of heaven, and the one hundred fifty day duration.

The formal translation does not adjudicate source questions but preserves the divine-name pattern of the MT exactly and in full: "the LORD" for יְהוָה and "God" for אֱלֹהִים, with no harmonization. The alternation is a feature of the received text, and formal equivalence demands that it be rendered accurately. The single verse where both divine names appear (v. 16) is translated so that both are visible: "as God had commanded him. And the LORD shut him in."

The juxtaposition in verse 16 of the divine names within a single verse is the most theologically charged instance in the chapter. The boarding of the ark is described by the P-style notation using Elohim ("as God had commanded him"), and then the intimate act of sealing the door is attributed specifically to יְהוָה ("and the LORD shut him in"). The transition from Elohim to YHWH at this precise moment — the closing of the door — is not incidental. See the detailed note on verse 16 below.

**2. טָהוֹר / לֹא טָהֹר (tahor / lo tahor) — "clean" / "not clean" (v. 2)**

Verse 2 is the locus of one of the most significant theological notes in the pre-Sinai narrative: the distinction between clean and unclean animals is here assumed as already known to Noah, centuries before the Mosaic legislation of Leviticus 11 and Deuteronomy 14 formally codifies it.

The Hebrew reads: מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה תִּקַּח לְךָ שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה — "From every clean beast take for yourself seven seven" (i.e., seven pairs). The distinction between הַטְּהוֹרָה (ha-tehorah, "the clean") and the animals described as אֵינֶנָּה טְהֹרָה (einennah tehorah, "she is not clean") is stated with formal precision.

The formal translation renders הַטְּהוֹרָה as "clean" and לֹא טָהֹר / אֵינֶנָּה טְהֹרָה as "not clean." The Hebrew avoids the word טָמֵא (tame, "unclean" — the word used throughout Leviticus for ritual impurity), using instead the negation of the positive: "not clean." This is the most formally precise rendering: the text does not call these animals "unclean" (which would import the full Levitical technical term) but "not clean" — a designation by negation rather than by positive label. Several formal equivalence translations collapse this distinction to "unclean" (KJV, ESV, NASB), but the Hebrew negation is specific and the distinction matters: the text carefully avoids the technical term טָמֵא at this pre-Sinai point.

The pre-Sinai existence of the clean/unclean distinction raises a significant exegetical question: where did Noah learn this distinction? The text does not explain. It either assumes a pre-Mosaic oral tradition of clean/unclean categories (which Leviticus then formalizes), or it reflects the narrator's retroactive application of categories the reader is assumed to know, or it is a feature of the J source's earlier theological framework. The formal translation does not resolve this question but preserves the text's own terminology accurately so that the question remains visible to the reader.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this verse with the standard Greek distinction καθαρά / μὴ καθαρά ("clean" / "not clean") — matching the MT's positive/negative structure precisely: the LXX similarly avoids rendering the negative animals as ἀκάθαρτα ("unclean," the technical Levitical equivalent) and uses the negated form μὴ καθαρά instead.

**3. שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה (shiv'ah shiv'ah) — "seven pairs" / "seven seven" (vv. 2–3)**

The Hebrew of verse 2 reads: שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה — the numeral seven repeated. Hebrew uses this distributive reduplication to indicate "seven each" or "seven and seven" — i.e., pairs within a group of seven, or simply "seven of each sex." The formal translation renders this "seven pairs" (following ESV, NASB), which captures the distributive sense. However, the Hebrew is literally "seven seven" — not "seven pairs" explicitly. The number seven here applied to male-female pairs produces fourteen animals per clean species (seven males, seven females), or alternately seven pairs. The formal translation chooses "seven pairs" as the most coherent English rendering of the distributive reduplication, following the traditional interpretation.

This stands in apparent tension with Genesis 6:19–20, which commands "two of every kind." The Documentary Hypothesis identifies this as a seam between the P account (Genesis 6:19–20: two of every kind) and the J account (Genesis 7:2–3: seven pairs of clean, two of unclean). The formal translation preserves both commands without harmonization, as both appear in the received text.

**4. מַבּוּל (mabbul) — "flood" (vv. 6, 7, 10, etc.)**

The Hebrew word מַבּוּל (mabbul) is used throughout the flood narrative as the specific technical term for the Noahic flood. It is a distinctive word — not the general term for flood (שֶׁטֶף, sheteph, or נָהָר, nahar) but a specialized term that appears in the Hebrew Bible only in Genesis 6–11 and in Psalm 29:10 (where the LORD "sits enthroned over the flood" — veyeshev YHWH lemabbul — and the reference is almost certainly to the Noahic flood). The word may be related to an Akkadian or other Semitic root, but its precise etymology is debated.

The formal translation renders מַבּוּל as "flood" throughout, which is the standard formal equivalence rendering and preserves the specificity of the term. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders מַבּוּל as κατακλυσμός (kataklusmos) — "flood, deluge, inundation" — the Greek word from which the English "cataclysm" derives. The LXX rendering is an interpretive equivalence rather than a formal one: κατακλυσμός is a descriptive term (from κατά + κλύζω, "to wash over") rather than a technical term, though it accurately conveys the scale.

**5. בְּשֶׁנַת שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה לְחַיֵּי נֹחַ (v. 11) — The Flood Date Formula**

Verse 11 contains one of the most precise date notations in the entire Hebrew Bible: "In the six-hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month." The notation uses the form: year / month / day — the standard ancient Near Eastern date formula. This level of chronological precision is characteristic of the P source's interest in exact dating (compare the precise dates of the flood chronology in Genesis 8:13–14 and the tabernacle construction calendar in Exodus 40:17).

The phrase בְּיוֹם הַזֶּה — "on that very day" (literally "on this day," emphasizing the exactness of the moment) — frames the event as a specific, historically located occurrence. The formal translation renders this "on that very day" following the idiomatic emphasis of the Hebrew.

The date "second month, seventeenth day" has generated extensive discussion in Jewish and Christian tradition regarding which calendar is in view. The debate is between: (1) a spring-New Year calendar (Nisan = month 1, making the second month = Iyyar, with the flood beginning in May), and (2) an autumn-New Year calendar (Tishri = month 1, making the second month = Marcheshvan, with the flood beginning in November). The formal translation does not identify the specific month by name but renders the number ("second month") following the MT exactly.

**6. כָּל מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה / אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם (v. 11) — Cosmological De-creation: "Fountains of the Great Deep" and "Windows of the Heavens"**

Verse 11 is the theological and cosmological center of the flood narrative. Two distinct cosmic mechanisms are activated simultaneously: נִבְקְעוּ כָּל מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה ("all the fountains of the great deep burst open") and וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ ("the windows of the heavens were opened").

These two phrases represent a deliberate reversal of the creation order of Genesis 1. In Genesis 1:6–8, God created the raqia (firmament) to separate the waters above from the waters below, creating the bounded space in which life could exist. In Genesis 7:11, both the waters above (released through the windows of the heavens) and the waters below (bursting through the fountains of the great deep) are released simultaneously — a structural, cosmological unmaking of the creation order. The flood is not merely a meteorological event: it is a cosmic de-creation in which the primordial waters of Genesis 1:2 reassert themselves through the rupture of every boundary.

The phrase תְּהוֹם רַבָּה (tehom rabbah, "the great deep") deliberately echoes תְּהוֹם (tehom) from Genesis 1:2 — "the deep" that covered the earth before the Spirit of God moved over the waters. The same deep is now bursting open from below. The noun תְּהוֹם is cognate with the Babylonian Tiamat, the primordial sea-monster of the Enuma Elish creation epic; though the text does not mythologize the deep, the cosmological resonance of the term is present and intentional.

The phrase אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם (arubboth hashamayim, "windows of the heavens") uses the noun אֲרֻבָּה (arubbah) — a latticed opening, a window, a sluice. The word is used in 2 Kings 7:2, 19 for a "window of heaven" from which food would pour (in a metaphor for miraculous abundance), and in Malachi 3:10 for the windows through which God pours out blessing. Here the same windows are opened for judgment: the same cosmic apertures that channel divine blessing now channel divine destruction.

The formal translation renders: "all the fountains of the great deep burst open, and the windows of the heavens were opened." This preserves the cosmological register of the Hebrew and its explicit connection to the Genesis 1 creation vocabulary. "Firmament" (רָקִיעַ, raqia) does not appear in chapter 7 itself, but the "windows of the heavens" presuppose the firmament-structure of Genesis 1:6–8 and connect to it directly; the cosmological de-creation reading of verse 11 depends on that connection being legible to the reader.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: πᾶσαι αἱ πηγαὶ τῆς ἀβύσσου ("all the springs of the abyss") and αἱ καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἠνεῴχθησαν ("the floodgates/cataracts of the heaven were opened"). The LXX uses καταρράκτης (kataraktes, "floodgate, cataract, waterfall") for אֲרֻבָּה — a more hydraulic, forceful image than "window." The LXX's rendering captures the violent, unleashed character of the event, where the MT's "window" (arubbah) is more architectural. The formal translation follows the MT's "windows" rather than the LXX's "floodgates."

**7. וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ (vayyisghor YHWH ba'ado) — "the LORD shut him in" (v. 16)**

Verse 16b is one of the most theologically arresting clauses in the entire flood narrative: וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ — "and the LORD shut him in." After all the meticulous loading of the ark — the animals, the family, the provisions — described in the preceding verses using the more formal "God" (Elohim), it is the LORD (YHWH) who personally, directly closes the door.

The verb סָגַר (sagar) means "to shut, to close, to lock." The preposition בַּעֲד (ba'ad) means "behind, around, for the sake of, in behalf of" — it carries a protective sense: to close something around someone is to enclose them, to seal them in protectively. The phrase is not merely "the LORD shut the door" but "the LORD shut [him] in" — the suffix is third person masculine singular, referring to Noah (or possibly to the whole group as represented by Noah). The divine act of closing is an act of intimate, personal protection. The LORD himself seals Noah inside.

This is the divine-name shift of maximum intimacy: the boarding of the ark is described in the universal, architectural terms of Elohim ("as God had commanded him"), and then at the moment of closing — the moment of final separation between the preserved and the perishing — it is YHWH who acts. The personal, covenantal name of God is used for the personal, covenantal act of sealing the covenant partner inside the vessel of salvation.

The formal translation renders: "And the LORD shut him in." The brevity of the clause in English mirrors the brevity of the Hebrew. Nothing is added; nothing is softened. The LORD shut him in. It is one of the great sentences of the Hebrew Bible.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: καὶ ἔκλεισεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν κιβωτὸν ἔξωθεν αὐτοῦ — "and the Lord God shut the ark from outside him" (or: "shut the ark on the outside"). The LXX adds ὁ θεός ("God") to κύριος ("Lord"), producing the combined double-name κύριος ὁ θεός (Lord God), and specifies that the ark was shut "from outside" (ἔξωθεν). The LXX's addition of "from outside" makes explicit what the MT leaves implicit: the LORD shut the door from the outside, not Noah from within. The formal translation follows the MT's more compressed and intimate formulation.

**8. רוּחַ חַיִּים / נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים (vv. 15, 22) — "Breath of Life" / "Breath of the Spirit of Life"**

Two related but distinct phrases describe the animating principle in chapter 7:

- Verse 15: רוּחַ חַיִּים (ruach chayyim) — "the breath of life" (literally: "breath/spirit of life")
- Verse 22: נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים (nishmat ruach chayyim) — "the breath of the spirit of life" (literally: "breath of breath/spirit of life" — a construct chain of two related life-terms)

The verse 22 phrase נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים is one of the most theologically dense phrases in the flood narrative. It combines two distinct words for breath/life: נְשָׁמָה (neshamah, the breath God breathed into Adam in Genesis 2:7 — נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים) and רוּחַ (ruach, spirit/wind/breath). The combination of both words in a single construct chain is unique in the Hebrew Bible. The neshamah is the animating divine breath of Genesis 2:7; the ruach is the more general term for the spirit/life-force. Together they describe what every land creature in whose nostrils this double-breath dwells: everything in whose nostrils is the most fundamental animating principle of creaturely life.

The formal translation renders verse 15 as "the breath of life" and verse 22 as "the breath of the spirit of life" — preserving the compound construct chain of the Hebrew without collapsing it. "Everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life" (v. 22) reflects the Hebrew literally and is the most formally faithful rendering. The ESV renders "in whose nostrils was the breath of life" at both verses, collapsing the distinction. The KJV renders v. 22 as "in whose nostrils was the breath of life" likewise. The NASB renders v. 22 as "the breath of the spirit of life" — which is the rendering followed here.

The scope of the devastation is marked by the phrase מִכֹּל אֲשֶׁר בֶּחָרָבָה מֵתוּ — "from all that was on dry land, died" (v. 22). The word חָרָבָה (charabah) is used for dry land as opposed to water — the domain of terrestrial life. Everything on the dry land with the breath of the spirit of life in its nostrils died. This is total annihilation of terrestrial life outside the ark.

**9. וַיִּמַח אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם (vayyimach et kol ha-yequm) — "every living thing was blotted out" (v. 23)**

The verb מָחָה (machah) — "to blot out, to wipe out, to erase" — is used in verse 23 for the destruction of all land life: וַיִּמַח אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם — "and He blotted out every living thing." The same verb was used in Genesis 6:7, where the LORD announced: "I will blot out the human beings whom I have created." The verb machah carries the sense of erasing what is written — as one wipes a tablet clean — and of destroying what exists so thoroughly that it leaves no trace. The formal translation renders it "blotted out" at both 6:7 and 7:23, maintaining lexical consistency.

The word יְקוּם (yequm) — "living thing, that which exists upright, creature" — is a relatively rare term for living beings. The formal translation renders it "living thing" following the most common formal equivalence tradition.

The verse then restates the scope in ascending/descending enumeration: "from human being to beast, to creeping thing, to birds of the heavens." This list moves from the highest category of terrestrial life (human beings) down through beasts, creeping things, and birds — completing the reversal of the creation enumeration from Genesis 1. In Genesis 1, life was created from simpler to more complex, culminating in the human being; in Genesis 7:23, the blotting out is enumerated from the human being downward. The formal translation preserves this order.

**10. וַיִּשָּׁאֵר אַךְ נֹחַ (vayyishshaer akh Noach) — "And only Noah was left" (v. 23)**

The climactic pivot of the chapter: וַיִּשָּׁאֵר אַךְ נֹחַ — "And only Noah was left." The particle אַךְ (akh) is the particle of restriction — "only, exclusively, nothing but." After the comprehensive blotting-out of all terrestrial life, the sole survivor is identified with this particle of restriction: only Noah, and those with him in the ark.

The formal translation renders: "And only Noah was left, and those that were with him in the ark." The Hebrew וַאֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה — "and those that were with him in the ark" — follows as the extension of the restriction: the "only" encompasses Noah and his ark-company together. The tebah (תֵּבָה, ark) appears here as the defining boundary: the line between the blotted-out and the remaining runs exactly along the walls of the ark.

The word תֵּבָה (tevah, "ark, chest, box") is used in the Hebrew Bible only for the Noahic ark (Genesis 6–9) and for the basket/chest in which the infant Moses was placed in the Nile (Exodus 2:3, 5). The two uses are almost certainly connected by deliberate literary parallel: the tevah that saves Noah from the waters of judgment and the tevah that saves Moses from the waters of the Nile are structurally equivalent — vessels of divine preservation floating on waters that kill all around them. The formal translation renders תֵּבָה as "ark" following the standard convention; the Moses parallel is noted here.

**11. וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם (vayyigb'ru hamayim) — "the waters prevailed" (vv. 18–20, 24)**

The verb גָּבַר (gavar) — "to prevail, to be strong, to overcome, to be mighty" — is used repeatedly in the flood narrative: verses 18, 19, 20, and 24. The verb carries the sense of water actively overcoming the resistance of the terrain — the waters do not merely rise but prevail, overpower, overcome. The formal translation renders גָּבַר throughout as "prevailed," maintaining lexical consistency across the chapter.

The escalating intensification of verses 18–20 is structurally significant:
- Verse 18: וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם וַיִּרְבּוּ — "And the waters prevailed and increased greatly"
- Verse 19: וְהַמַּיִם גָּבְרוּ מְאֹד מְאֹד — "And the waters prevailed exceedingly greatly" (the double מְאֹד מְאֹד, "exceedingly, exceedingly," intensifying the superlative)
- Verse 20: חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה מִלְמַעְלָה גָּבְרוּ הַמָּיִם — "Fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed"

The triple use of גָּבַר in verses 18, 19, and 20, with escalating intensity adverbs, is the formal Hebrew literary device of intensification through repetition and augmentation. The formal translation preserves all three uses of "prevailed" and preserves the double מְאֹד מְאֹד as "exceedingly greatly" at verse 19, rendering the escalation visible in English.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 7

The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 7 was read directly. The following variants and divergences from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | כִּי אֹתְךָ רָאִיתִי צַדִּיק לְפָנַי ("for I have seen that you are righteous before me") | ὅτι σὲ εἶδον δίκαιον ἐναντίον μου ("for I have seen you righteous before me") | "for I have seen that you are righteous before me" | LXX matches MT closely; formal equivalence preserves "before me" (לְפָנַי / ἐναντίον) as positional idiom |
| 7:2 | מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהוֹרָה שִׁבְעָה שִׁבְעָה ("from every clean animal, seven seven") | ἀπὸ δὲ τῶν κτηνῶν τῶν μὴ καθαρῶν δύο δύο ("from the unclean beasts two by two") | "seven pairs ... two" | LXX uses μὴ καθαρῶν ("not clean") matching MT's negation; LXX has distributive δύο δύο also for clean animals in some traditions |
| 7:4 | כִּי לְיָמִים עוֹד שִׁבְעָה ("for in yet seven days") | ἔτι γὰρ ἡμερῶν ἑπτά ("for yet seven days") | "in seven more days" | LXX matches MT; "in" added for English idiom |
| 7:11 | אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ ("windows of the heavens were opened") | οἱ καταρράκται τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἠνεῴχθησαν ("the floodgates/cataracts of heaven were opened") | "windows of the heavens" | LXX καταρράκτης ("floodgate/cataract") is more hydraulic/forceful than MT's architectural אֲרֻבָּה ("window/lattice"); MT followed |
| 7:16 | וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ ("and the LORD shut him in") | καὶ ἔκλεισεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὴν κιβωτὸν ἔξωθεν αὐτοῦ ("and the Lord God shut the ark from outside him") | "and the LORD shut him in" | LXX adds ὁ θεός (double-name) and ἔξωθεν ("from outside") — two expansions absent from MT; MT's compressed clause preserved |
| 7:22 | נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים ("breath of spirit of life") | πνοὴν ζωῆς ("breath of life") | "breath of the spirit of life" | LXX collapses the compound Hebrew construct chain to a simpler two-word phrase; MT's full construct chain preserved |
| 7:23 | וַיִּמַח אֶת כָּל הַיְקוּם ("and He blotted out every living thing") | καὶ ἐξήλειψεν πᾶν τὸ ἀνάστημα ("and He wiped out every standing thing") | "every living thing was blotted out" | LXX ἐξαλείφω ("to wipe off, to efface") matches MT's machah in sense; ἀνάστημα ("standing thing, upright creature") renders יְקוּם with a different nuance — the erect posture of creatures rather than the living character |
| 7:24 | וַיִּגְבְּרוּ הַמַּיִם עַל הָאָרֶץ חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם ("the waters prevailed upon the earth one hundred and fifty days") | καὶ ὑψώθη τὸ ὕδωρ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἡμέρας ἑκατὸν πεντήκοντα ("and the water was raised upon the earth one hundred and fifty days") | "one hundred and fifty days" | LXX uses ὑψόω ("to raise/lift up") rather than prevail; both agree on 150-day duration |

---

### DSS Note — Genesis 7

The Dead Sea Scrolls contain fragmentary evidence relevant to Genesis 7:

- **4QGenb (4Q2)**: Fragmentary Genesis manuscript from Qumran Cave 4. Preserves portions of Genesis 6–7. The extant fragments of 4QGenb in the Genesis 7 range are limited but where they survive they agree substantially with the MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex, with no significant textual variants.
- **4QGene (4Q5)**: Another fragmentary Genesis manuscript from Cave 4, covering portions of the early chapters of Genesis. The fragments in the Genesis 7 range are minimal and do not yield independent variants.
- **4QGenesisd (4Q4)**: Fragmentary Genesis, Cave 4. No complete Genesis 7 verses preserved; the fragments that touch this chapter do not contradict the MT.
- **1QapGen (1Q20, the Genesis Apocryphon)**: This Aramaic expansion of the Noah narrative (cols. VI–XVII) covers the flood story in an elaborated retelling. The Genesis Apocryphon does not represent an independent manuscript tradition of the biblical text but rather a literary paraphrase and expansion. The Apocryphon's Noah narrative includes: a first-person account from Noah, the description of Noah's great distress during the flood, and expansions of the cosmological language. The Apocryphon's flood dates differ slightly from both the MT and LXX chronologies, suggesting it follows a distinct calendrical tradition (possibly the 364-day solar calendar of the Qumran community). The Apocryphon's description of the fountains of the deep and the cosmic waters in the flood is considerably more elaborate than the MT's compressed verse 11, demonstrating that the cosmological significance of this verse was recognized and expanded in Second Temple exegesis.
- **Jubilees (4Q216–224)**: The book of Jubilees, fragments of which are preserved at Qumran, contains a retelling of the flood narrative in chapters 5–7 that uses a 364-day solar calendar and gives precise dates for the flood's beginning, the resting of the ark, and the exit from the ark. The Jubilees dates do not match either the MT or LXX flood chronology exactly, but they engage with the same narrative. Jubilees 5:23 has Noah entering the ark on the seventeenth day of the second month, matching the MT's date of verse 11 exactly.

---

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 7 performs three interlocking literary and theological functions within the flood narrative as a whole: it executes the divine command of Genesis 6, it enacts the cosmological de-creation, and it identifies the sole surviving remnant through whose preservation the creation order is maintained.

**1. The Divine Command Fulfilled (vv. 1–9)**

The chapter opens with the LORD's direct command to Noah (v. 1) and closes the first movement with the compliance formula: "And Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him" (v. 5). This is the second compliance formula in the flood narrative — the first appeared at Genesis 6:22 ("And Noah did according to all that God commanded him; so he did"). The repetition is structurally significant: Genesis 6:22 closes the Elohim-commanded section of ark construction; Genesis 7:5 closes the YHWH-commanded section of boarding. Both formulae use the same root (עָשָׂה, asah, "to do, to make") and both affirm total compliance. The double compliance formula frames Noah's obedience as comprehensive and unreserved.

The divine command of verse 1 is given in the voice of the LORD (YHWH), the covenantal name, and it opens with an invitation: בֹּא — "Come." The imperative בֹּא (bo, "come") is the imperative of entry from within — not "go into the ark" (the command would then use לֵךְ, lekh, "go") but "come into the ark." The use of בֹּא rather than לֵךְ implies that the LORD speaks from within the ark — inviting Noah to join him in the place of safety. This grammatical detail, widely noted in the Hebrew tradition, is theologically charged: the LORD is already present in the ark; Noah is invited to enter into the divine presence. The formal translation renders it "Come" following the MT imperative exactly.

**2. The Cosmological De-creation and its Reversal (vv. 10–20)**

Verses 10–20 are the cosmological center of the flood narrative. The escalating language of the waters — bursting, opening, pouring, prevailing, increasing, mounting — corresponds to a systematic reversal of Genesis 1:

| Genesis 1 | Genesis 7 |
|---|---|
| 1:2 — "darkness over the face of the deep" | 7:11 — "fountains of the great deep burst open" |
| 1:6–7 — firmament separates waters above from below | 7:11 — windows of heaven opened; the separation undone |
| 1:9–10 — dry land appears; waters bounded | 7:19–20 — all the high mountains covered; dry land gone |
| 1:20–25 — birds, animals, creeping things created | 7:21 — birds, livestock, beasts, creeping things expire |
| 1:26–27 — human beings created | 7:21–23 — human beings blotted out |

The flood is a structural inversion of the creation: each creative act of Genesis 1 is undone by a corresponding act of de-creation in Genesis 7. The firmament that bounded the upper waters (Gen 1:6) is breached (7:11 — the windows are opened); the dry land that was separated from the waters (Gen 1:9) is submerged (7:19–20); the living creatures of each category are destroyed in reverse order (7:21–23) matching their creation in forward order (Gen 1). The formal translation preserves the vocabulary connections between chapters 1 and 7 wherever they appear — particularly the shared use of the same terms for "living creatures," "birds of the heavens," "creeping things," and the "face of the ground."

**3. The Remnant and the LORD's Closure (vv. 16b, 23–24)**

The theological climax of the chapter is the structural juxtaposition of universal annihilation (vv. 21–23) and singular preservation (v. 23b). The chapter moves from the largest possible scope — all flesh, every living thing, everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life — to the smallest possible remnant: אַךְ נֹחַ ("only Noah").

Between the universality of the destruction and the singularity of the survivor stands the brief, unadorned sentence of verse 16b: וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ — "And the LORD shut him in." The divine act of closure — intimate, personal, YHWH-specific — is the narrative hinge on which the entire chapter turns. The LORD who grieves over the wickedness of human beings (6:6), who invites Noah to "come" into the ark (7:1), and who shuts the door behind him (7:16) is the same LORD who will later open the windows and dry up the earth (Genesis 8). The flood narrative is not a story of divine indifference or mechanical cosmic justice: it is a story of divine grief, divine protection, divine closure, and ultimately divine remembrance (8:1 — "And God remembered Noah"). The closing of the door in 7:16 is the seal of that remembrance: the LORD has enclosed his covenant partner in the vessel of preservation, and what the LORD shuts no flood can open.

— LOGOS
